title: Saṃtānāntarasiddhi
author: Dharmakīrti
summary: A short treatise ('Proof of the Existence of Other Mindstreams/Continua') addressing the Buddhist version of the problem of other minds. Dharmakīrti argues that knowledge of other sentient beings' minds is inferred from observed bodily movements and speech that one knows, by analogy with one's own case, to be caused by intention; he further argues that the Yogācāra idealist can justify belief in other minds by the same argument from analogy that the realist uses.
history: One of Dharmakīrti's two minor works (alongside the Sambandhaparīkṣā), difficult to place within the sequence of his seven epistemological treatises. The Sanskrit is largely lost; the text survives chiefly in Tibetan translation and was the subject of a commentary by Vinītadeva.

title: Pramāṇavārttikaṭīkā
author: Śākyabuddhi
summary: A detailed sub-commentary (ṭīkā) on Dharmakīrti's Pramāṇavārttika, the foundational verse-text of Buddhist epistemology and logic. Śākyabuddhi glosses Dharmakīrti's arguments on valid cognition (pramāṇa), perception, inference, the apoha theory of meaning, the unreality of universals, and debate technique against Nyāya and Brahmanical opponents.
history: Śākyabuddhi (often dated c. 700 CE) was an important early exegete of Dharmakīrti; his ṭīkā was in turn drawn upon by Karṇakagomin. The Sanskrit survives only in fragments (e.g. National Archives Kathmandu manuscripts transcribed in 1992); the full text is preserved in Tibetan translation.

title: Vṛttamālāstuti
author: Jñānaśrīmitra
tradition: Buddhist (Mahāyāna; author of the pramāṇa tradition)
summary: A 'Garland-of-Metres Hymn': a praise-poem to the Bodhisattva Mañjuśrī composed across roughly 150 different Sanskrit metres, where each stanza simultaneously praises Mañjuśrī and exemplifies (and in effect defines) the metre in which it is cast. It thus serves both as devotional stotra and as a practical handbook of Sanskrit prosody.
history: Jñānaśrīmitra was a celebrated Buddhist philosopher-poet and dvāra-paṇḍita of Vikramaśīla monastery. The work was transmitted with Śākyarakṣita's commentary (Vṛttamālā(stuti)vivṛti) and has been critically edited by Michael Hahn, S. S. Bahulkar, and others.

title: Chandoratnākara
author: Ratnākaraśānti (Śāntipa)
summary: An 'Ocean of Metres', a treatise on Sanskrit (and Prakrit) prosody that describes metres and sets out combinatorial algorithms (pratyaya) for enumerating metrical patterns, including binomial-type enumeration. It treats both classical metres and Prakrit/Apabhraṃśa material.
history: Composed by Ratnākaraśānti, an 11th-century Buddhist master of Vikramaśīla. Conceived partly as a reworking in the tradition of Piṅgala's Chandaḥsūtra, it was influential on prosody in Nepal and on the Buddhist metrical culture transmitted to Tibet.

title: Siddhārthacaritrakāvya
author: Buddhaghoṣa (Buddhaghoṣa Thera, modern Sri Lankan author)
genre: mahākāvya (court epic poem) on the life of the Buddha
summary: A Sanskrit kāvya narrating the life-story (caritra) of Siddhārtha Gautama, the future Buddha. As a 'Siddhārtha-conduct poem' it recounts in ornate verse the Buddha's birth, renunciation, awakening and career.
history: Attributed to a Buddhaghoṣa who is not the famous 5th-century Pali commentator but apparently a later (modern Sinhalese) monk-poet; the work is preserved in modern digital Sanskrit Buddhist collections. Attribution and dating are not securely established, so identification is tentative.

title: Yaśodharācarita
author: Davuldeṇa Jñāneśvara (Davuldena Gnanissara) Mahāsthavira
summary: A modern Sanskrit narrative poem on the life of Yaśodharā, the wife of Prince Siddhārtha (the future Buddha), telling her story, marriage, separation and devotion within the broader Buddha-legend.
history: Composed by the Venerable Davuldena Gnanissara Mahānāyaka Thera, an eminent Sri Lankan scholar-monk and centenarian (1915-2017) renowned for his command of Sanskrit, Pali and English. The work is an example of modern Sinhalese Sanskrit literary composition, preserved in digital Sanskrit-text collections.

title: Triratnasaundaryagāthā
summary: Verses (gāthā) celebrating the beauty/excellence (saundarya) of the Three Jewels (triratna): the Buddha, the Dharma, and the Saṅgha. It is a devotional praise-composition extolling the qualities of the three Buddhist refuges.
history: Anonymous in the available sources; transmitted in modern digital Sanskrit Buddhist text collections. It could not be confidently dated or attributed, and may be a modern composition; identification is tentative.

title: Saṅgītamālikā
genre: kāvya / gīti (lyrical poetry, possibly a 'garland of songs')
summary: A 'Garland of Songs/Music', apparently a collection of lyrical Sanskrit verses or songs on Buddhist themes. The precise content and arrangement could not be confirmed from available sources.
history: Anonymous in available catalogues and preserved in modern digital Sanskrit Buddhist text collections; very likely a modern (Sinhalese) Sanskrit composition. The work could not be confidently identified, so the description is tentative.

title: Paścāttāpa
summary: A poem titled 'Repentance/Remorse' (paścāttāpa), treating the theme of contrition and moral self-reproach. The exact narrative content could not be confirmed; the title signals a Buddhist meditation on repentance.
history: Anonymous in available sources and preserved in modern digital Sanskrit Buddhist text collections; very likely a modern Sinhalese Sanskrit composition. Could not be confidently identified, so the description is tentative.

title: Saundarananda Mahākāvya
author: Aśvaghoṣa
summary: A mahākāvya in 18 cantos narrating the conversion of Nanda, the Buddha's handsome half-brother. The first half depicts Nanda's worldly life and his love for his wife Sundarī and his reluctant ordination; the second half expounds Buddhist doctrine, meditation and the ascetic path leading to Nanda's awakening as an arhat.
history: Composed by Aśvaghoṣa, a Buddhist monk-poet of c. 1st-2nd century CE associated with Sāketa/Ayodhyā. Together with his Buddhacarita it is among the earliest surviving examples of Sanskrit kāvya and a model for later poets including, arguably, Kālidāsa.

title: Kriyāsaṃgrahakārikā
author: attributed to Nāgārjuna (uncertain; not the Madhyamaka founder)
genre: kārikā (verse manual) on ritual (kriyā) and consecration
date_estimate: uncertain; likely late 1st millennium / early 2nd millennium CE
summary: A verse compendium ('Summary of Ritual Actions') setting out tantric Buddhist ritual procedures, especially the consecration (pratiṣṭhā) of images and stūpas and associated construction rites. It belongs to the body of Vajrayāna ritual literature dealing with the establishment and worship of sacred objects.
history: Attributed to a 'Nāgārjuna' (a name used by many later tantric authors, not the Madhyamaka philosopher). It is closely related to the Kriyāsaṃgrahapañjikā of Kuladatta, a fuller ritual manual whose consecration material parallels the Śaiva pratiṣṭhātantras. Authorship and date are not securely established.

title: Samyaksambuddhabhāṣita Pratimālakṣaṇa
author: anonymous/traditional (ascribed to the Buddha's word)
date_estimate: archetype c. Gupta period - 10th c. CE (before Atiśa, d. 1054)
summary: The 'Characteristics of Images as Spoken by the Perfectly Awakened One', a canonical text laying down the proportional measurements (tālamāna / aṅgula units) for making images of the Buddha. It specifies the correct body-proportions for sculpted and painted Buddha figures and is foundational to Buddhist iconometry.
history: One of the principal sources for image-proportion theory in the Tibetan Buddhist canon (where it circulates as the 'Sūtra requested by Śāriputra'), most likely belonging to the Mūlasarvāstivāda tradition. The Sanskrit archetype probably dates to the Gupta period, no later than the 10th century; it was rendered into Tibetan in the early 14th century.

title: Ātreyatilaka
tradition: Indian (śilpaśāstra; iconometry, used in Buddhist art context)
summary: A short text on the proportional measurements (lakṣaṇa) of images, transmitted alongside the corpus of iconometric/pratimālakṣaṇa literature. It belongs to the technical tradition of tālamāna prescribing how the parts of a sacred image should be measured.
history: A minor and obscure iconometric text grouped with the Pratimālakṣaṇa-family of image-proportion manuals; preserved in collections of iconometric literature. Its author, date and exact relations are not securely established, so identification is tentative.

title: Pratimālakṣaṇavivaraṇa
summary: A commentary (vivaraṇa) explaining the Pratimālakṣaṇa, the canonical text on the proportions of Buddha-images. It elaborates the tālamāna/aṅgula measurement system used to construct correctly proportioned images of the Buddha.
history: The Vivaraṇa on the Pratimālakṣaṇa was translated from Sanskrit into Tibetan by Grags-pa-rgyal-mtshan and the Indian paṇḍita Dharmadhara; it is a recognized part of the Tibetan iconometric corpus. Its Indian author is not securely identified.

title: Citralakṣaṇa
author: Nagnajit (traditional attribution)
tradition: Indian (śilpaśāstra; influential in Buddhist art traditions)
genre: śilpaśāstra / citraśāstra (treatise on painting and image-proportions)
date_estimate: c. Gupta period (5th-6th c. CE) or earlier
summary: One of the oldest treatises on the canons of Indian painting and image-making, ascribed to the sage Nagnajit. It sets out the principles of portraiture, iconometry (proportional measurements), and the rules for depicting figures, framed in a dialogue/origin narrative of the art of painting.
history: The original Sanskrit was largely lost to the Indian tradition; the work survives chiefly through a Tibetan translation (studied since Berthold Laufer's 1913 edition). It is of major importance for early Indian art history and for the painting/iconometric traditions of Tibet, Mongolia and China, and must be distinguished from the later Citralakṣaṇa chapter in Śrīkumāra's 16th-century Śilparatna.

title: Stūpalakṣaṇakārikāvivecana
genre: vivecana (explanatory commentary) on verse-rules for stūpa proportions
summary: An explanatory work ('Discussion of the Verses on the Characteristics of Stūpas') treating the prescribed proportions and construction features of the Buddhist stūpa. It expounds the kārikās governing the measurements and symbolic structure of stūpas, whose parts are mapped onto the body of the Buddha and the five elements.
history: A specialized text in the Buddhist architectural/iconometric tradition concerned with stūpa proportions (transmitted especially in Tibetan-influenced sources). Its author and precise date are not securely established, so identification is tentative.

title: Pratyakṣalakṣaṇa
genre: philosophical treatise / definitional text on perception
summary: A short text on the 'Definition/Characteristics of Perception' (pratyakṣa) within Buddhist epistemology. Following the Dignāga-Dharmakīrti tradition, it would define perception as an unmistaken, non-conceptual (kalpanāpoḍha) cognition whose object is the bare particular (svalakṣaṇa), distinguishing it from inference (anumāna).
history: Anonymous in the available sources; the work_id suggests transmission alongside Tarka (logic) literature. It treats the standard Buddhist definition of perception elaborated in works such as Dignāga's Pramāṇasamuccaya and Dharmakīrti's Nyāyabindu. The specific text could not be confidently identified, so the description is tentative.

title: Śatagāthā
author: Vararuci (traditional attribution)
summary: A 'Hundred Verses' (also known as the Gāthāśataka), a collection of around 100-107 moral and gnomic maxims (nīti). The verses convey ethical and worldly-wisdom teachings drawing on the Prajñādaṇḍa and Cāṇakya nīti traditions.
history: Attributed to Vararuci and included in the Tibetan Tanjur; the work survives primarily in Tibetan translation. It belongs to the popular Buddhist didactic-aphorism literature transmitted across South and Inner Asia.

title: Nītiśāstra
author: attributed to Masūrākṣa / Nāgārjuna (traditional, uncertain)
summary: A 'Treatise on Right Conduct/Policy', a collection of gnomic verses on ethics, prudent conduct, and worldly wisdom. It belongs to the Buddhist nīti tradition of moral aphorisms (akin to the Cāṇakya/Bhartṛhari nīti collections) preserved in the Sanskrit Buddhist corpus.
history: Transmitted anonymously or under traditional ascriptions (such as to Masūrākṣa or 'Nāgārjuna') in Buddhist nīti collections, and preserved in digital Sanskrit Buddhist text canons. The specific recension and authorship are not securely established, so identification is tentative.

title: Tarkabhāṣāṭīkā
author: Śrīmokṣākaragupta (text) / commentary by a later ṭīkākāra
date_estimate: after c. 1100 CE (base text late 11th-12th c.)
summary: A commentary (ṭīkā) on the Tarkabhāṣā ('Language of Logic') of Mokṣākaragupta, one of the finest late summaries of Buddhist logic and epistemology. The base Tarkabhāṣā concisely surveys the pramāṇa system (perception, inference, the apoha theory, and debate), and this ṭīkā glosses and elaborates its arguments.
history: Mokṣākaragupta was one of the great paṇḍitas of the Jagaddala monastery in Varendrī (North Bengal), founded under King Rāmapāla c. 1080 CE; his Tarkabhāṣā represents one of the last systematic works of the Dharmakīrtian tradition before the decline of Buddhism in India. The ṭīkā is a later exegetical layer on this manual.
